content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


August 1, 2001


Guy D. Petzold
Deputy City Attorney
City of Stockton
Office of the City Attorney
425 North El Dorado Street
Stockton, CA 95202-1997

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-141

Dear Mr. Petzold:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

Is Mayor Gary Podesto disqualified from participating in a decision about the Duck Creek Project?

CONCLUSION

No. Mayor Podesto is not disqualified from participating in a decision about the Duck Creek Project under the Act because he has no economic interest in the decision.

_____________
1 Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-01-141
Page No. 2

FACTS

There is currently pending before the city council an application for a general plan
amendment and rezoning of approximately 106 acres from industrial to residential
(known as the "Duck Creek Project"). The developer of Duck Creek is John Verner. A
neighboring industrial company named California Spray Dry has expressed opposition to
the project. The opposition is based on a concern that if the city council approves the
project, the new homeowners will complain about the odors from California Spray Dry.

Modesto Tallow Company owns California Spray Dry. The president of Modesto
Tallow Company is Jeff Podesto, who is the second cousin of Mayor Gary Podesto. The
mayor has no financial interest in either Modesto Tallow Company or California Spray
Dry. The only connection between the mayor and Jeff Podesto is their family
relationship.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official knows or has reason to know he or she has a financial
interest. (Section 87100.) Section 87103 of the Act provides that "a public official has a
financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family²,
or on any of the following:

a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or
indirect investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more³.
b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.
c) Any source of income, other than gifts or loans by a commercial
lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided
to, promised to, or received by, the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.

___________________________
2 "Immediate family" is defined in Section 82029 as the official's spouse and dependent children.
3 For purposes of Section 87103, "indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned
by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a
business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater."

File No. A-01-141
Page No. 3

e)    Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or
gifts aggregating three hundred twenty dollars ($320) or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made."
(Section 87103, subdivisions a – e.)

A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal finances. A
governmental decision has a personal financial effect on a public official if the decision
will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the public official, as
well as those of his or her immediate family, increasing or decreasing by an amount of
$250 or more. This is known as the "personal financial effect" rule (Section 87103;
Regulations 18703.5 and 18705.5).

You have indicated Mayor Podesto has no financial interest in either Modesto
Tallow Company or California Spray Dry. You have also provided no information
suggesting that decisions on the application would have a personal financial effect on
Mayor Podesto. The relationship between the mayor and Jeff Podesto, who is not a
member of the mayor's "immediate family," does not independently create an economic
interest in Jeff Podesto. Therefore, Mayor Podesto is not disqualified from participating in
a decision about the Duck Creek Project.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature: Kelly L. Winsor]

By:    Kelly L. Winsor
Legal Analyst

No, Mayor Podesto is not disqualified from participating in a decision about the
Duck Creek Project under the Act because he has no economic interest in the decision.
--,
